title: Evangelion The Prisoner and LOST 2004
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion, The Prisoner, and LOST (2004)             Quote Post by MrGeekBoi » Thu Mar 24, 2016 4:08 pm As I said in another post, the end to the series Neon Genesis Evangelion reminds me very much of the end to The Prisoner. Both confounded their audiences with something that seemed a complete departure from the rest of the series, yet which does make some sense within the context of the framework. Both inspired hate-mail, and both inspired the fans who "got it." I absolutely love what Anno says about the ending of his show, that they will never directly reveal the answers to the mystery because it's a puzzle, that no one in life is going to hand you the answers to everything and that you need to seek/find the answers for yourself. This is a great philosophy for storytelling. Yes, there are certainly types of stories that deserve a direct explanation, a beginning, a middle and an end. Detective stories, for instance, would feel pretty flat if we never found out who actually dunnit (even within this framework there are still the occasional story where the point is not to find out who did it, certain episodes of Homicide, for instance or the first season of True Detective). Also many sci-fi/fantasy/space operas like Star Wars are definitely going to have definitive outcomes. These are to be expected. But every great once in a while a story or series comes along that captures the audiences' imagination, and requires no explanation. The Prisoner was one of the first of these stories - at least when it comes to the scripted TV format. In it, an unnamed secret agent (many believe him to be Drake from the TV series "Secret Agent") resigns from the British Service and is promptly abducted, waking up in The Village, a community of ex-spies from across country lines. Everyone is a number, with the new arrival being designated Number 6. Unable to tell which side (if any) runs the village, Number 6 evades attempts by the leader of the Village, Number 2 (with the eternal question being "Who is Number 1?"), to learn why Number 6 quit. "My reasons are my own" is a sentiment echoed by Anno when explaining the ending of Evangelion. Incidentally, Number 2 changes from episode to episode within the series, hinting at the fluid nature of politics and states in the 60s when this was made. The end of The Prisoner is a head trip that needs to be seen to be experienced. I could hardly do it justice to try to describe it here, and it would spoil it for new viewers were I to do so (it's on Blu-ray kiddies and it looks like it was filmed yesterday!). Take it from one who knows, the last 2 episodes are existential journeys that have no comparison. The closest analogue would be Lost, the creators of which cite The Prisoner as one of their major influences. They also like Stephen King, another proponent of the "don't-show-what's-behind-the-door" style of storytelling. Now, while the end of Lost is still quite a bit existential, it is much more straightforward than Evangelion or The Prisoner - although the main meat of the story is, conversely, even more enigmatic than Eva or The Prisoner was, surely in an attempt to build up and extend the show's central mysteries. So, what am I getting at here? To be honest, I don't really know. Mainly I'm just working out my first impressions of the final episodes of Eva. The ending is so much like The Prisoner's end it's uncanny, and maybe I'm just trying to reach out and see if there's anyone here who feels the same way? I'd also like to express that I thought the story was great and hopefully will be recognized as the philosophical tour de force it is.

[]: Re: Evangelion, The Prisoner, and LOST (2004)             Quote Post by MrGeekBoi » Thu Mar 24, 2016 5:52 pm I thought of a good point regarding all three - the subversion of genre expectations. All three series represent this in varying degrees. The Prisoner, for instance, was on the surface a spy-fi series that utilized gadgets and plot devices that wouldn't be out of place in a James Bond thriller. However, they often subverted the genre, having the antagonists use these devices on the protagonist, with him never in control. Eva often subverts the anime genre. It has bright cheery music over domestic scenes, like the sound cue that appears whenever Shinji and Pen Pen are eating breakfast together. These sound cues and other plot devices lead the viewer to believe the show is going to go one way, until episode 15-18, which completely pull the rug out from under you and turn the narrative and much darker and more disturbing (not to mention at times depressive) direction. Definitely not the expectations viewers would have after years of InuYasha, Ranma 1/2, Robotech and Gundam shows. Lost is a little different in this regard. While they do often subvert genre expectations, it also is a completely unique concept. It starts out as a dramatic narrative, shifting over time to being a thriller, and finally straying into science fiction territory. Once again, going back to Stephen King as an influence, they structured the series much the way King structures his horror novels - starting out with familiar, dramatic situations, then making the familiar strange. In all three cases, the series subvert the expectations of the audience, and in not providing all the answers, refusing to spoon-feed the audience, they take bold strides by letting the piece speak for itself, and allowing to audience to find their own answers.

[]: Re: Evangelion, The Prisoner, and LOST (2004)             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Mar 24, 2016 6:33 pm MrGeekBoi wrote: In it, an unnamed secret agent (many believe him to be Drake from the TV series "Secret Agent") resigns from the British Service

[]: Re: Evangelion, The Prisoner, and LOST (2004)             Quote Post by MrGeekBoi » Thu Mar 24, 2016 7:06 pm "the Lost Decade didn't have the "something in the air" of the Swinging Sixties -- which makes it the more remarkable." While I did not experience the 60s firsthand, I always felt there to be something in the late 90s that was somewhat akin to the revolutionary spirit of the late 60s. No, there wasn't a great war, but there certainly was an artistic explosion. Music, in particular in the 90s was pushing at new boundaries, much the way music in the 60s had been. 1969 and 1999 both saw an amazing cinema explosion. Not to mention, Evangelion in a way predicted 9/11 and global warming. Not specifically, but the Second Impact occurs in 2000, making 2001 "pure hell" as described in "...He was Still a Child." In a way, the tail end of 2001 was pure hell for America. And now, here in 2015-2016 we are seeing 2 of the hottest years on records, with the polar ice caps threatening to melt if we don't curb the planet's carbon consumption. In addition, I remember feeling in the late 90s (and a lot of friends who felt the same way) that something big was just around the corner, something that would change the world - and it wasn't just because of the new millennium rising. All this is a bit of a roundabout way to say I always felt the late 90s were a very good time for the cinematic, TV and music arts.
